images.caption: Figure 1:Comparison of Gaussian allocation under different target Gaussian budgets.(a) Given the same target Gaussian budget,F4\text{F}^{4}Splat allocates Gaussians non-uniformly using predicted densification scores, avoiding over-allocation in simple regions and concentrating primitives on fine-detail regions (e.g., flowers) to better preserve details even under a small budget, in contrast to voxel-based uniform allocation (AnySplat[jiang2025anysplat]). (b) Compared to pose-free and uncalibrated baselines,F4\text{F}^{4}Splat achieves the best reconstruction fidelity (LPIPS/PSNR), while largely preserving the rendering quality as the number of Gaussians is reduced.

Figure 2:Overview ofF4\text{F}^{4}Splat.Given multi-view context images, the Geometry Backbone predicts camera parameters, multi-scale Gaussian parameter maps, and densification score maps. During training, camera and Gaussian parameters are jointly optimized with the camera lossℒcamera\mathcal{L}^{\text{camera}}, rendering lossℒrender\mathcal{L}^{\text{render}}, and scene-scale regularizationℒscene\mathcal{L}^{\text{scene}}. The predicted densification score maps are trained by score lossℒscore\mathcal{L}^{\text{score}}derived from the backpropagation of rendering loss. The final representation𝒢τ\mathcal{G}_{\tau}, constructed using a randomly sampled thresholdτ\tau, is also supervised by the rendering lossℒrender\mathcal{L}^{\text{render}}.
At inference time, the predicted Gaussian parameter maps and densification scores are used to generate compact, high-fidelity 3D Gaussian representations𝒢τN¯𝒢\mathcal{G}_{\tau_{\bar{N}_{\mathcal{G}}}}tailored to user-specified Gaussian budgetsN¯𝒢\bar{N}_{\mathcal{G}}. This adaptation is efficient and does not require retraining.

Figure 3:Spatially adaptively density allocation.Given two context images (left), the model predicts densification score maps that estimate where additional Gaussian density is required.
Theredcolor indicates where more Gaussians are placed based on different fixed thresholdsτ\tau.
In the top example (RealEstate10K[zhou2018re10k]), we can clearly observe that more Gaussians are allocated in complex regions. The example below (ACID[liu2021acid]) demonstrates redundancy-aware allocation across overlapping views, avoiding unnecessary allocations.

Figure 4:Calculatingℒ𝒢score\mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{G}}^{\text{score}}.We convert the view-space rendering gradient𝐯g\mathbf{v}_{g}for each𝐠g∈𝒢\mathbf{g}_{g}\in\mathcal{G}into a densification scoredgd_{g}and learn the network to predict it.

Figure 4:Calculatingℒ𝒢score\mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{G}}^{\text{score}}.We convert the view-space rendering gradient𝐯g\mathbf{v}_{g}for each𝐠g∈𝒢\mathbf{g}_{g}\in\mathcal{G}into a densification scoredgd_{g}and learn the network to predict it.
Figure 5:Effect of the thresholdτ\tauacross scene complexity.For a fixedτ\tau, complex scenes yield higher densification scores and allocate more Gaussians, while simpler scenes allocate fewer.

Figure 6:Qualitative comparisons of novel view synthesis on RE10K.The bottom-right corner of each image shows the PSNR of the rendered view and the number of Gaussian primitives used for scene reconstruction.
Our method achieves high-quality rendering even with substantially fewer primitives, while consistently outperforming competing approaches.
Figure S1:Additional qualitative comparisons of novel view synthesis on RE10K[zhou2018re10k]dataset.
